question: A process is deployed that has a signal start event for the signal named `OrderCancelled`. What does the deployment create, and what happens when `OrderCancelled` is later broadcasted?

options.text: Nothing is created at deployment; only running process instances can react to a broadcast.
A signal subscription is created at deployment; broadcasting `OrderCancelled` triggers the subscription and starts a new process instance.
A correlation key must be supplied with the broadcast; without it the broadcast is buffered until a process instance starts.
The signal start event is registered only on the partition that owns the deployment record; broadcasts on other partitions are ignored.

a.text: The docs state that deploying a process with a signal start event creates a new signal subscription at deploy time, not only at instance creation.

b.text: Per the Signal subscriptions section, deploying a process with a signal start event creates a new signal subscription, and a matching broadcast triggers it to start a new process instance.

c.text: Signals match by name only — they do not use a correlation key, unlike messages.

d.text: Signal subscriptions are not partition-local; signals are broadcast to all partitions.

explanation: The Signal subscriptions section explains that a signal catch event creates a subscription when it is reached at runtime, while a signal start event creates its subscription at process deployment time. When a signal with a matching name is broadcasted, the start-event subscription is triggered and a new process instance is started. Matching is by signal name only — no correlation key is involved.
